request.question: Where are the studios, where the Muppet Shows were recorded, located ?

title: Michelan Sisti
The Muppet Show
Muppet*Vision 3D
Babilla (album)
Eamonn Andrews Studios
Countdown (game show)
WFAA Communications Center Studios
BBC Elstree Centre
De Lane Lea Studios
Mama Melrose's Ristorante Italiano
sentences: Michelan Sisti (born May 27, 1949) is a Puerto Rican-American actor, director, puppeteer, and musician who played Michelangelo in "Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles" and its sequel "".
He previously had an eighteen-year theatrical career including five Broadway shows.
Since "TMNT", Micha moved from New York City to Los Angeles and has continued to work with The Jim Henson Company, Walt Disney Studios, and many other movie and television productions.
September 8–10, 2017, he was an additional Muppet performer for a live show at the Hollywood Bowl titled "The Muppets Take the Bowl".
The Muppet Show is a family-oriented comedy-variety television series that was produced by puppeteer Jim Henson and features The Muppets.

After two pilot episodes produced in 1974 and 1975 failed to get the attention of network executives in the United States, Lew Grade approached Henson to produce the programme in the United Kingdom for ATV.

The show lasted for five series consisting of 120 episodes which were first broadcast in Britain between 5 September 1976 and 15 March 1981 on ATV and was shown by the other ITV franchises in the United Kingdom.
The programs were recorded at Elstree Studios, England.
Muppet*Vision 3D is a 3D film attraction located at Disney's Hollywood Studios.

Directed by Jim Henson, the attraction features Kermit the Frog guiding park guests on a tour through Muppet Studios, while the Muppets prepare their sketch acts to demonstrate their new breakthrough in 3D film technology.
The show, however, completely unravels when Dr. Bunsen Honeydew's experimental 3D sprite, Waldo, causes mayhem during the next portion of the show.
Babilla is the thirteenth studio album by Puerto Rican rapper Vico C, released on October 6, 2009 through Caribbean Records under exclusive license to EMI Televisa.

The 69% of the album was recorded at Luny Tunes' Más Flow Studios in Carolina, Puerto Rico, while the remaining 31% was recorded at Pichaera Studios, Golden Boy Studios (both in San Juan, Puerto Rico) and Rotary Music Studios (Bayamón, Puerto Rico).
Vico C produced almost the entire album, while Hyde, Luis Almonte and Joel Morales worked as recording engineers.
Half the album was mixed at KDS Studios, while American producer Mike Fuller mastered it at Fullersound Inc., both studios located in Florida.
All tracks feature session musicians for guitars, drums, trumpet, trombone, saxophone and choirs, while Vico C played the electronic keyboard on eight of the thirteen songs that includes the album.
Eamonn Andrews Studios was founded by broadcaster Eamonn Andrews, famous for being the presenter with the big red book of "This Is Your Life," a British television programme.
In the 1960s, the launch of television in Ireland led him to become the Chairman of the RTÉ Authority.

He set up a media production company in Ireland, the Eamonn Andrews Studios, which ran a dance hall called The Television Club in Harcourt Street in Dublin and took on the lease of the Gaiety Theatre in Dublin.
Eamonn Andrews Studios were originally located at 40 Henry Street in Dublin.
In the 1960s the company bought the TV Club building on Harcourt Street.
It was located directly opposite, what was then, the Old Harcourt Street Station.
It housed two distinctly different studios.
A radio studio which recorded sponsored programmes for Radio Éireann, and commercials, and a band studio down stairs.
It used the dance hall during the day to record all the main bands of the Showband era.
At nighttime, the bands were showcased live on stage in what the company called "The Mecca of Irish Nightlife".
Countdown is a British game show involving word and number puzzles.
It is produced by ITV Studios and broadcast on Channel 4.
It is currently presented by Nick Hewer, assisted by Rachel Riley, with regular lexicographer Susie Dent.
It was the first programme to be aired on Channel 4, and 77 series have been broadcast since its debut on 2 November 1982.

With over 6,500 episodes, "Countdown" is one of the longest-running game shows in the world, along with the original French version, "Des chiffres et des lettres" (Numbers & Letters), which has been running on French television continuously since 1965.
"Countdown" was initially recorded at The Leeds Studios for 27 years, before moving to Granada Studios in 2009, and then over to MediaCityUK in Salford Quays in 2013.
WFAA Communications Center Studios are the main studio operations of Tegna, Inc. owned television station WFAA, which is an ABC affiliate, located at 606 Young Street in Dallas, Texas.
WFAA-TV is the only station in the Dallas-Fort Worth broadcasting market to not become a network O&O station.
The studio opened in 1961, and housed WFAA-TV, WFAA-AM, and WFAA-FM, later known as KZEW-FM.
From 1938 to 1970, WFAA-AM was both an NBC and ABC station which meant that the building housed both the NBC and ABC networks.
The station that WFAA shared networks with was WBAP-AM which was housed at Broadcast Hill in Fort Worth.
In the early years, WFAA was more known to Dallas and WBAP was more known to Fort Worth.
WFAA-TV played a strong part in youthful programs such as Sump'n Else, The Group And Chapman, Peppermint Place, and many others.
During the 1960s, WFAA-AM continued to broadcast entertainment, but its ratings were weakened due to Top 40 station KLIF-AM's format.
The KLIF Triangle Point Studios are located a few blocks from WFAA in Downtown Dallas.
WFAA Studios are a major part of Dallas-Fort Worth broadcasting history.
At one time, Downtown Dallas had three different broadcast headquarters, which are WFAA, KRLD, and KLIF.
While many national television shows were on tour, they occasionally filmed a few segments at WFAA Studios.
BBC Elstree Centre, sometimes referred to as BBC Elstree Studios, is a television production facility located on Clarendon Road in Borehamwood, Hertfordshire.
De Lane Lea Studios is a recording studio, currently based in Dean Street, Soho, London, England, UK.

Although the studios have mainly been used for dubbing feature films and television programmes, major artists such as The Animals, the Beatles, Soft Machine, Queen, the Rolling Stones, Bee Gees, The Who, the Jimi Hendrix Experience, Pink Floyd, Renaissance, Electric Light Orchestra and Deep Purple recorded songs in their studios, particularly at their former premises at 129 Kingsway  and at Engineers Way, Wembley, where Queen recorded demos in 1971.
After De Lane Lea, these studios were taken over by Lee Lighting which then, many years later, became the Fountain Studios.
It is now home to shows like "The X Factor".
Mama Melrose's Ristorante Italiano is a restaurant in the Grand Avenue area at Disney's Hollywood Studios.
Located near Muppet*Vision 3D and Star Tours, the restaurant specializes in Italian cuisine, serving such dishes as "ossobuco", wood-fired flatbread pizza, and grilled salmon.
A "Fantasmic!
" dinner package is available that grants restaurant guests quicker access to this show.
This package is also offered at two other restaurants in the park: the Hollywood Brown Derby and Hollywood & Vine.
Ron Douglas included the recipe for Mama Melrose's cappuccino "crème brûlée" in his cookbook "America's Most Wanted Recipes: Just Desserts".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Where are the studios, where the Muppet Shows were recorded, located ?
hop1_docs: Sam Eagle | Sam Eagle is a Muppet character originating from the television show "The Muppet Show", where he was performed by Frank Oz.  Sam has appeared in every Muppet film; as himself in "The Muppet Movie", "The Great Muppet Caper", "The Muppets Take Manhattan", "Muppets from Space", and "The Muppets", as well as the Head Schoolmaster in "The Muppet Christmas Carol," Samuel Arrow in "Muppet Treasure Island" and a CIA agent in "Muppets Most Wanted."  He also appears in the television series, "The Muppets."

The Muppet Show | The Muppet Show is a family-oriented comedy-variety television series that was produced by puppeteer Jim Henson and features The Muppets.  After two pilot episodes produced in 1974 and 1975 failed to get the attention of network executives in the United States, Lew Grade approached Henson to produce the programme in the United Kingdom for ATV.  The show lasted for five series consisting of 120 episodes which were first broadcast in Britain between 5 September 1976 and 15 March 1981 on ATV and was shown by the other ITV franchises in the United Kingdom.  The programs were recorded at Elstree Studios, England.

Dr. Teeth and The Electric Mayhem | Dr. Teeth and The Electric Mayhem is a Muppet rock house band that debuted on "The Muppet Show".  Following "The Muppet Show", they appeared in various Muppet movies and television specials and have also recorded album tracks and covered numerous songs.  Dr. Teeth and Animal were designed by Jim Henson while the rest of the original band members were designed by Michael K. Frith.  They made their debut in 1975's "" and the pilot for "The Muppet Show".

The Muppets discography | The discography of The Muppets, an American puppet group originally created by puppeteer Jim Henson, includes of fifteen studio albums, four compilation albums, one extended play, eight singles, two "featured artist" singles and five music videos.  Of the studio and compilation albums, three are soundtracks from seasons of "The Muppet Show" and seven of eight motion pictures.  The soundtrack to the sixth film, "Muppets from Space" was orchestral as opposed to derivative compositions performed by the characters.  Walt Disney Records has served as the primary record label for musical projects by The Muppets since 2004.

Muppet*Vision 3D | Muppet*Vision 3D is a 3D film attraction located at Disney's Hollywood Studios.  Directed by Jim Henson, the attraction features Kermit the Frog guiding park guests on a tour through Muppet Studios, while the Muppets prepare their sketch acts to demonstrate their new breakthrough in 3D film technology.  The show, however, completely unravels when Dr. Bunsen Honeydew's experimental 3D sprite, Waldo, causes mayhem during the next portion of the show.

Gonzo (Muppet) | Developed and performed by Dave Goelz, Gonzo made his first appearance in the 1970 special "The Great Santa Claus Switch", as the "Cigar Box Frackle".  Originally a minor figure in "The Muppet Show", he soon evolved into one of the franchise's primary characters.  Gonzo has appeared in every Muppet film, including "The Muppet Christmas Carol", where he portrayed author Charles Dickens and developed a double act with Rizzo the Rat.

The Muppet Show (comics) | The Muppet Show is comic book based on the variety television series "The Muppet Show" and featuring The Muppets.  The series was written and drawn by Roger Langridge and published by Boom Kids! , an imprint of Boom!  Studios.  In 2011, the Boom!  license with Disney Publishing Worldwide expired.  Disney's own comic book publishing subsidiary, Marvel Comics, renamed the series "Muppets" and published four issues in 2012.
hop1_titles: Dr. Teeth and The Electric Mayhem
response.summary_1: Evidence: passage states the programs "were recorded at Elstree Studios, England." Bridge entity: Elstree Studios. Missing fact: the studio's precise location (town and county or full address) is not given in the passages. Retrieval directions: 1) Open the "Elstree Studios" Wikipedia page (or Elstree Studios official site) to confirm its exact location (town, county, address). 2) If needed, search "Elstree Studios location site:wikipedia.org" for authoritative location details.
response.hop2_query: Open https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elstree_Studios and extract the studio's location (town and county; include full address if listed).
hop2_docs: Robert Jessup | Robert Jessup (born July 18, 1952) is an American painter.  Although currently working in the abstract style he painted for most of his career in the figurative style (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Figurative_art), particularly in large triptych panels (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Triptych).
List of aircraft carriers of World War II | https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=List_of_aircraft_carriers_of_World_War_II&action=edit

Shariat-e-Halal Accreditation Trust | Shariat-e-Halal Accreditation Trust is founded on 9 June 2017 by Dr. Syed Kalbe Sadiq (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kalbe_Sadiq) with Mufti Abul Irfan Firangi Maheli with three other Individuals to provide input of Sharia'h Law to Quality Council of India to create Halal Accreditation Scheme for Halal Certification Bodies.

Semien Achefer | Semien Achefer or ("North Achefer")or ("Liben https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raghopur_Narsanda_Panchayat") is one of the woredas in the Amhara Region of Ethiopia.  It is named for the historic district of Achefer, which was first mentioned in the 16th century.  Part of the Mirab Gojjam Zone, Achefer is bordered on the south by Debub Acheref, on the west by the Semien Gondar Zone, on the north by Lake Tana, on the east by Bahir Dar Zuria, and on the southeast by Mecha; the Lesser Abay River defines the woreda's eastern boundary.  The woreda includes Dek Island.  The administrative center is liben; other towns in Semien Achefer include Yesmala, Dembola,Sankira & Kunzila.  Semien Achefer was part of former Achefer woreda.

Brit Miller | Brit William Miller (born September 15, 1986) is a former American football fullback. He was born in Decatur, Illinois.  He went to high school at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eisenhower_High_School_(Decatur,_Illinois). He was signed by the Carolina Panthers as an undrafted free agent in 2009.  He played college football at Illinois.  Miller also played for the San Francisco 49ers, and the St. Louis Rams.  He was waived by the Rams on November 20, 2012.
hop2_titles: List of aircraft carriers of World War II
response.summary_2: The Muppet Shows were recorded at Elstree Studios in Borehamwood, Hertfordshire, England. Elstree Studios is located in the town of Borehamwood in the county of Hertfordshire, north of London.
